decisions.title: Agent spec 'model:' field semantics
decisions.context: Agent spec YAMLs may declare a model field with or without a provider prefix. packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1173-1179 implements parseModelSpec such that bare model ids (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6') default to provider='anthropic'. This structural pin means monitor classify calls attempt Anthropic-direct authentication regardless of the main session's configured provider. Under OpenRouter-only auth (the user's current configuration), every monitor's classify call fails with 'Expected either apiKey or authToken'. Issue-062 tracks this as the forcing artifact. All five bundled classifier agent YAMLs (hedge, commit-hygiene, fragility, work-quality, unauthorized-action) use bare model ids and are all affected. The decision is what 'model:' means in an agent spec at the framework level.
decisions.decision: Bare model ids in an agent spec resolve against the current session's configured provider via ExtensionContext. If ExtensionContext cannot supply a current-provider/current-model binding, the dispatch errors with a clear message naming the missing context. Fully-qualified model specs (provider/model) continue to pin the provider regardless of session. Pinning is explicit, not a default. Implementation prerequisite: research on ExtensionContext currentModel availability in pi-agent-core must complete first.
consequences: pi-behavior-monitors' classifyViaAgent must read the current session model from ExtensionContext at dispatch time, not from the agent YAML
parseModelSpec semantics change — bare specs route through ExtensionContext lookup instead of returning provider='anthropic' as a default
The five bundled classifier YAMLs can remain with bare model ids and will now follow whatever provider the user has configured
If ExtensionContext does not currently expose currentModel, that becomes a prerequisite unblocker — opens a scope question into pi-agent-core that must be verified before implementation
Workflow per-step provider override (issue-064) becomes the symmetric problem for workflow agents; same resolution applies

references.label: DEC-0001 enactment + citation refresh (parseModelSpec line numbers 1091-1097 → 1173)

decisions.title: Thinking-seam enforcement for forced-toolChoice dispatch
decisions.context: Agent spec YAMLs can declare thinking: on, but packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1449-1450 does not forward thinking to the complete() call. The comment at lines 1449-1450 region acknowledges that the Anthropic API rejects thinking + forced toolChoice, so thinking must stay off when the dispatch uses a phantom-tool verdict shape. All five bundled classifier YAMLs declare thinking: on despite the silent drop. Issue-063 tracks this dead config as the forcing artifact. The framework does not currently enforce consistency between spec-declared thinking and dispatch-time behavior.
decisions.decision: executeAgent in pi-jit-agents (once parseModelSpec and classify dispatch move there per DEC-0003) enforces thinking=off whenever toolChoice.type === 'tool' is present in the dispatch options. If the agent spec declares thinking: on in combination with forced toolChoice, the framework emits a warning naming the agent and the incompatibility, and proceeds with thinking disabled. Free-form (non-forced) dispatches continue to forward thinking per the spec. The forced-toolChoice path never silently drops thinking without notification.
consequences: executeAgent gains a pre-dispatch check that inspects toolChoice and the compiled spec's thinking field
The five bundled classifier YAMLs should be corrected to thinking: off for cleanliness, separately from the enforcement change
Any future agent that combines forced toolChoice with thinking: on will be flagged loudly rather than silently mis-executing

Warning channel emits a TraceEntry via the canonical pi-jit-agents trace-writer pipeline (packages/pi-jit-agents/src/trace-writer.ts + agent-trace-sdk.ts). NOT via DispatchContext (attestation metadata, wrong layer) and NOT via JitAgentResult (typed return surface, wrong concern). Per feedback_no_stderr_diagnostics: diagnostics route through the canonical TraceEntry pipeline; the original 'logger via DispatchContext OR diagnostic via JitAgentResult' or-hedge is resolved by canon to the TraceEntry form (2026-05-26 narrowing).

references.label: DEC-0002 enactment + consequence #4 narrowing (or-hedge → TraceEntry) + citation refresh

decisions.title: parseModelSpec ownership at the execute boundary
decisions.context: parseModelSpec lives in packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts today. It defaults bare model ids to provider='anthropic', pinning the monitor classify path to Anthropic regardless of session configuration. The same function logic (model-id parsing and provider selection) belongs at the execute boundary, which pi-jit-agents now owns per docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md §2. Keeping it in pi-behavior-monitors creates duplication (issue-043, the forcing artifact) and embeds policy in the wrong layer. pi-workflows has the same structural question for its agent-step dispatch but with different currently-hidden behavior. Widening 2026-05-26: empirical grep verified a SECOND parseModelSpec duplicate at packages/pi-workflows/src/step-monitor.ts:371 (used at line 270) — DEC-0003 originally said 'audit pi-workflows / if found consolidate'; this widening converts the audit-conditional into a confirmed-two-site cleanup. Both duplicates are within scope of the move; neither is to be left behind.
decisions.decision: Move parseModelSpec into pi-jit-agents as part of the executeAgent boundary. Remove the CONFIRMED duplicates at TWO sites: (a) packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1173 (definition) + 1390 (call site); (b) packages/pi-workflows/src/step-monitor.ts:371 (definition) + 270 (call site). The pi-workflows duplicate is empirically verified (2026-05-26 widening), not merely 'to audit' — DEC-0049 uniform-agent axiom forbids the duplication and TASK-080 dismantles agent-layer copies in the same direction. Update the consumers' classifyViaAgent (monitors) and step-monitor's classify dispatch (workflows) to call the pi-jit-agents API. The function's new location must accommodate DEC-0001 (ExtensionContext lookup for bare specs) and DEC-0002 (thinking-seam enforcement).
consequences: pi-jit-agents gains model-spec-parsing inside the executeAgent dispatch path — as an internal (non-exported) helper at the executeAgent boundary, NOT as a fifth exported surface beyond load/compile/execute/introspect. The four-boundary-surfaces declaration of jit-agents-spec.md §2 stands; the 'either helper / or inlined' hedge in the original consequence is resolved to the inlined-or-internal-helper form by canon (jit-agents-spec §2 four-surface declaration + JI-010 composite-not-decomposition).
pi-behavior-monitors' classifyViaAgent shrinks — it calls executeAgent with a compiled spec and an ExtensionContext, no longer doing its own provider resolution
pi-workflows' step-monitor classify dispatch shrinks identically — the duplicate parseModelSpec at step-monitor.ts:371 is removed; line 270 redirects to the pi-jit-agents API
Requires DEC-0001 resolved before implementation, because the bare-spec semantics determine what parseModelSpec's new contract is
After enactment + implementation: zero parseModelSpec definitions outside pi-jit-agents; verifiable via grep 'function parseModelSpec' returning a single hit in jit-agents only

decisions.title: TraceEntry schema independence from SessionEntry
decisions.context: Issue-023 requires side-car trace capture for monitor classify calls. Pi-mono's SessionManager.SessionEntry (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/session-manager.d.ts) carries the canonical tree structure (type discriminator, id, parentId, timestamp, extension fields). The architectural question: should TraceEntry inherit SessionEntry literally, or adopt its structural contract without tight coupling? Inherit-literally couples our trace evolution to pi-mono's session schema, so any upstream change auto-breaks traces. Adopt-structurally preserves pi-mono's tree-traversal patterns while keeping trace concerns scoped to the trace subsystem. The 2026-04-26 mandate-bound canonical-compliance audit established that the structural contract is canonical pi-mono and the inheritance is not — pi-coding-agent does not export SessionEntry as a base type for extension reuse, so structural adoption is the only path that does not invent a new pi-mono surface.
decisions.decision: Trace entries adopt the SessionEntry conceptual shape (type discriminator, id, parentId, timestamp, extension fields per kind) but live in a separate schema namespace. No literal inheritance, no shared base type. The trace subsystem owns its own discriminated union: session_start, classify_call, classify_response, context_collection, verdict_decision, trace_end. Both subsystems use identical timestamp semantics (ISO 8601 via new Date().toISOString()). If session schema evolves, traces continue independently.
consequences: TraceEntry is defined in pi-jit-agents and consumed by pi-behavior-monitors; not inherited from pi-coding-agent
Trace entries pass the same tree-traversal queries as session entries (getEntry / getBranch shape) if ever unified for analytics; that integration is opt-in and scoped to extension code
Both subsystems use identical timestamp semantics so cross-subsystem time correlation works without conversion
Redaction filter operates on both session and trace entries via a shared comprehensive pattern set
Schema evolution is independent: changes to SessionEntry do not force changes to TraceEntry and vice versa
A new .workflows/schemas/agent-trace.schema.json defines the trace shape; AJV validation reuses pi-project's schema-validator at write time

references.label: agent_end extension hook canonical capture point

decisions.title: Monitor observability via push-write trace stream (intentional divergence from pi-mono pull model)
decisions.context: Pi-mono SessionManager is pull-only: agents and extensions read session state via getBranch / getEntry / getEntries / getChildren but cannot write directly. SessionManager enforces readonly via the ReadonlySessionManager interface delivered to extensions (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/session-manager.d.ts). Monitors are side-channel executors — classify calls fire on turn_end and message_end events outside the main agent loop — and require independent write capability to record execution traces. Issue-023 names the capture requirement. The architectural question is whether to align with pi-mono's pull/replay observability model (structurally impossible given ReadonlySessionManager) or to diverge to a push-observability surface scoped to monitors. The 2026-04-26 mandate-bound canonical-compliance audit verified that pi-mono provides zero push-observability primitives (no Tracer, no Telemetry, no Logger framework, only event subscription) and that the divergence is the only path that satisfies the capture requirement without violating ReadonlySessionManager.
decisions.decision: Monitors implement a separate push-observable trace subsystem (TraceWriter at the pi-jit-agents executeAgent boundary) that does NOT attempt to write to the session. This is an intentional divergence from pi-mono's pull-observable architecture. The divergence is justified by three forces: monitors are not agents; classify events should not pollute session history; ReadonlySessionManager structurally prevents the pull-aligned alternative. The trace subsystem is scoped to pi-behavior-monitors and pi-jit-agents and does not generalize to other extensions. Future upstream pi-mono push-observable surfaces (if added) do not force refactor — the separation is intentional and stable.
consequences: Pi-mono remains the system of record for agent conversation and tool execution (session pull-model)
Monitors are the system of record for classification events (trace push-model)
Consumers of monitor health and classification patterns query the trace directory, not the session
Trace writes pass through schema validation extending pi-project's schema-validator pattern — net-new write-time validation discipline because pi-mono SessionManager has no equivalent

Trace data may include credentials echoed by users in prompts; pi-mono has no redaction primitives so the trace subsystem ships a comprehensive redaction layer covering all known credential shapes plus a project-extension mechanism for domain-sensitive data

Bounded growth via date-rotation (.workflows/monitors/<name>/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl), 30-day rolling retention, 500MB-per-day overflow split — mirrors pi-coding-agent's timestamp-anchored session-file naming
Configuration via PI_AGENT_TRACE_DIR, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILTER, PI_AGENT_TRACE_RETENTION_DAYS, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILE_MAX_SIZE_MB env vars adopting pi-mono's canonical PI_* prefix convention
CLI flags --trace, --no-trace, --trace-dir <dir>, --trace-filter <file> mirroring pi-coding-agent's boolean / negation / value-arg patterns

references.label: agent_end extension hook canonical capture point

decisions.title: Sixth block of the substrate contract
decisions.context: The user's framing names the substrate contract as 'six discrete blocks.' Five enumerated against POC v2 evidence (config, partitions, lenses, closure-table relations, per-item macros). The sixth block of the contract is unresolved. Pre-heuristic candidates from the conversation were schemas-as-substrate, hierarchies-as-distinct-block, or macros split into registry + emitter. The heuristic-widening pass (mandates + monitors as in-scope typed contexts under one heuristic) narrows preferred candidates to two: prompt-composition contract OR scopes (user / project / agent). Implementation cannot proceed with contract enumeration until this resolves.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. Two heuristic-aligned candidates compete; three pre-heuristic candidates retained for completeness but not preferred. Resolution updates §1.1 + §2 of analysis/2026-05-03-substrate-arc-frame.md and gates downstream block-enumeration.

options_considered.label: Prompt-composition contract
options_considered.description: The rules governing how typed contexts compose into prompts: order, deduplication, override, applicability evaluation, token-budget allocation across mandates + lens views + raw blocks + monitor outputs. Made central by the heuristic-widening; was implicit in the integration map.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Heuristic-aligned; central API surface for the unified frame. Couples sixth-block to gap #34 (composition contract).

options_considered.label: Scopes (user / project / agent)
options_considered.description: Layered anchors with composition rules across scopes. Mandates live at user scope; project blocks at project scope; agent overrides at agent scope. Composition resolves layered. Foundational for scope-layering decision DEC-0010.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Heuristic-aligned; couples sixth-block to gap #37 (scope layering). Selection here cascades into discovery-anchor mechanics.

options_considered.label: Schemas-as-substrate (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Every schema is itself a block, the meta-meta layer. Pre-heuristic candidate from POC v2 evaluation.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

options_considered.label: Hierarchies-as-distinct-block (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Hierarchy declarations elevate from a config field to their own typed block. Pre-heuristic candidate.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

options_considered.label: Macros-split (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Per-item macros + a separate cluster/whole-block render layer as separate blocks.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

consequences: Superseded: off-by-one re-count of the substrate contract. Original framing claimed six discrete blocks while enumerating five (config + partitions + lenses + closure-table relations + per-item macros). On reflection no sixth block exists; the contract is five blocks. Filed under proliferation pressure during the heuristic-widening pass; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: Composition contract for prompt assembly across typed contexts
decisions.context: Under the unifying heuristic, multiple typed-context entries (lenses, mandates, monitor outputs, raw blocks) can land in one agent's prompt. The integration-map design assumed pre-rendered injection without articulating how N typed contexts compose. Order, deduplication, override semantics, applicability-evaluation timing, and token-budget allocation are unspecified. This is the central API surface for the unified heuristic; lens injection, mandate injection, monitor-output injection all flow through it. Surfaced as gap #34 in the heuristic-widening pass.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. The composition contract must specify: (1) ordering rules across context types; (2) deduplication when multiple entries pull the same item; (3) override semantics including agent-scope opt-out of mandates / lenses; (4) applicability-predicate evaluation timing (compile-time vs per-turn); (5) token-budget allocation policy with truncation strategy. Resolution lands as a new design doc plus DEC enactment; gates implementation of jit-agents lens-injection path and downstream typed-context surfaces.
consequences: Superseded: composition rules for prompt assembly (ordering, deduplication, override, applicability evaluation, token-budget allocation) are specification work, not a directional choice between mandate-compliant alternatives. Belongs as an FGAP or implementation-issue series, not a DEC. Filed by treating 'things that need specification' as 'decisions to make'; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: contextBlocks parameterization: typed object form vs bare-string convention
decisions.context: The integration-map design proposed a bare-string `_lens:<id>` convention for lens-view injection in agent contextBlocks. The per-item-macros plan (analysis/2026-05-02-per-item-macros-atomic-plans.md plan #3) extends contextBlocks to a typed object form `{name, item, focus, depth}`. Two parallel parameterization mechanisms in one field. The heuristic-widening pass forces typed-only because bare-string cannot carry the parameter set the unified surface needs: `_mandate:<id>`, `_lens:<id>?status=open`, `_monitor:<id>:last-classification`, agent-scope omit lists, applicability filters. Bare-string would proliferate as `_kind:id:opt1:opt2` — a regression toward stringly-typed configuration. Surfaced as gap #29.
decisions.decision: Typed object form of contextBlocks entries (name + optional item/focus/depth) is the canonical injection surface for selective composition. Bare-string form (whole-block injection by name) remains valid for backward compatibility with existing agent YAMLs. The heuristic-widening forces the typed form because bare-string cannot carry the parameter set the unified composition surface needs (lens id, item selector, focus, depth, applicability predicate). Per-item-macros plan #3 is the canonical landing path.

options_considered.label: Typed object form only (proposed direction)
options_considered.description: Single typed surface. Carries kind, id, scope, applicability, focus, depth, filters. Bare-string remains valid for whole-block injection only.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Slightly more verbose at the simple case (whole-block injection unchanged; lens injection requires typed entry). Single canonical surface; no fragmentation.

options_considered.label: Bare-string convention layered on typed (coexistence)
options_considered.description: Both surfaces accepted. Bare-string for trivial cases; typed for parameterized.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Two surfaces to maintain. Author must learn when to use which. Composition contract (DEC-0007) more complex.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one canonical surface; coexistence creates the same parallel-ungated-path pattern the substrate arc retires.

consequences: per-item-macros plan #3 (typed contextBlocks extension) is the canonical landing path; no parallel bare-string convention
Lens-view injection lands as `{kind: 'lens', id: 'context-management', filters: {status: 'open'}}` not `_lens:context-management`
Mandate injection lands as `{kind: 'mandate', id: 'mandate-001'}` or `{kind: 'mandate', filter: {tags: ['workflow']}}`
Monitor output injection lands as `{kind: 'monitor', id: 'fragility', focus: 'last-classification'}`
Filter parameterization channel (gap #30) collapses into the typed form's `filters` field

decisions.title: FGAP-001 resolution: closure-table relations vs subpath block names
decisions.context: FGAP-001 (hierarchical/nested block storage) was originally framed with proposed_resolution = 'extend block-api to support subpath block names (e.g. readBlock(features/consumer-migration)).' The substrate-arc POC v2 demonstrates closure-table relations as an alternative substrate for hierarchy: (parent, child, relation_type) edges over flat blocks, with multiple parallel hierarchies coexisting as different relation_type values. The heuristic-widening pass (mandates and monitors as in-scope typed contexts) tilts decisively toward closure-table-only because the heuristic prefers ONE storage primitive across all context types — mandates and monitors do not need subpath storage.
decisions.decision: Closure-table relations are the canonical primitive for hierarchical decomposition over project blocks. Subpath block names (FGAP-001's original framing) are not pursued. The heuristic prefers ONE storage primitive across all context types (project blocks, mandates, monitor specs). The phases-pattern subdirectory walks (readBlockDir + buildIdIndex's phases handling) remain as legacy exceptions, not generalized. POC at analysis/poc-degree-zero-lens/ proves the closure-table shape mechanically; FGAP-001's resolution path is now closure-table-only via a relations.json block kind landing in pi-project.

options_considered.label: Closure-table relations only (proposed)
options_considered.description: All hierarchy expressed as edges in relations.json over flat blocks. One storage primitive across all context types per heuristic.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Per-item lock contention unchanged from today (per-block file lock). Hierarchy queries require edge-walk per query (mitigated by SDK caching). Authoring experience: edge-write rather than file-tree write.

options_considered.label: Subpath block names only (original FGAP-001 framing)
options_considered.description: readBlock('features/consumer-migration/stories/STORY-A'). Native tree shape on disk. Atomic per-item writes.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Two storage forms across the contract (subpath for hierarchy, flat-with-edges for everything else). Block-api gains directory-walk semantics. Schema-walk targeting array-item subschema is a new contract.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one storage primitive; subpath form would create a parallel ungated path for hierarchy expression that the closure-table primitive already handles.

options_considered.label: Both (closure-table for cross-cutting taxonomies, subpath for work-decomposition)
options_considered.description: Hybrid: closure-table for lens-style organization, subpath for genuine work-decomposition with atomic-write requirements.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Dual maintenance burden. Two query paths, two write paths, two validation paths.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one canonical substrate; hybrid creates the same fragmentation the unifying frame is meant to retire.

consequences: FGAP-001's proposed_resolution field updates to closure-table-only direction on enactment
Subpath block names not implemented; readBlockDir's existing one-off subdirectory walks (phases pattern) remain as exceptions, not generalized
Work decomposition (FEAT-001 epics → stories → tasks, future PHASE-NNN structures) lives as flat blocks with closure-table edges
Lock contention claim from FGAP-001 framing unchanged: per-block file lock; partition-per-file unblock is not pursued under this direction
block-validation.ts rollback contract simpler: no recursion into subdirectories

decisions.title: Scope discovery and layering across user / project / agent anchors
decisions.context: GitHub issue #3 asked for 'configuration for folder placement' — relocating .project/ + .workflows/. The POC v2 closes the data-relocation surface via config.root. The heuristic-widening pass widens this to a layered-scope decision because mandates live at user scope (~/.claude/mandates.jsonl) while project blocks live at project scope (<config.root>). Agent-scope overrides (e.g., classifier X opts out of mandates Y, Z) form a third layer. Composition rules across scopes are unspecified: which scope wins on conflict, how config-discovery walks scopes, whether the config file itself is relocatable (or fixed at .project/). Surfaced as gap #37, expansion of gap #24.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. Composition rule must specify: (1) discovery anchor for the config file itself — `.project/config.json` always vs env-var (PI_PROJECT_ROOT) vs package.json field vs walk-up; (2) layered config merge semantics across user → project → agent (project overrides user; agent filters within composed result, with explicit allow-list / deny-list); (3) scope assignment per block kind (mandates user-scope; project blocks project-scope; monitor specs project-scope or split). Resolution lands as DEC enactment plus updates to §1.1 and §5 of the substrate-arc-frame doc.

options_considered.label: `.project/config.json` always anchored
options_considered.description: Config file fixed at `.project/`; only data files relocate via config.root. Issue #3 closes for data, not for config-file location.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Predictable; no env-var discovery. User who literally wants no `.project/` directory at all not served.

options_considered.label: Env-var discovery (e.g., PI_PROJECT_ROOT)
options_considered.description: Config relocatable via environment. Substrate fully unanchored from `.project/`.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Requires env state; users who don't set the var get default `.project/`. Forking and CI environments must surface the var.

options_considered.label: package.json field (e.g., `pi.project.root`)
options_considered.description: Declarative; no env state. Lives in repo's package.json.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Couples pi config surface to npm package surface. Non-npm projects need an alternative.

options_considered.label: Walk-up discovery (find .project/config.json or named alternative in nearest ancestor)
options_considered.description: Discovery follows git-root convention; first-found wins.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Implicit anchor; may surprise users in nested-project contexts.

consequences: Superseded: the real concern (GitHub #3 folder placement) is already closed empirically by the POC's config.root field. The user/project/agent scope-layering expansion was theoretical embellishment from the heuristic-widening pass — not a question anyone asked. Mandates being user-scope is a fact, not a decision. Filed by lumping a closed folder-placement question together with scope-layering theory; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: Retire packaged defaults — pi-project ships no seed schemas or seed block files
decisions.context: pi-project today ships default schemas + default block files under packages/pi-project/defaults/{schemas,blocks}/, copied into newly-initialized projects by initProject (packages/pi-project/src/index.ts:215-236). The seeding pattern is a recurring source of architectural debt: stale seeded files cannot be updated by package upgrades. The original seedExamples copy-on-first-run pattern in pi-behavior-monitors had this exact failure mode and was retired in favor of live three-tier discovery (packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:325-352, doc-comment explicitly cites the retirement). Even pi-project's additive-only initProject creates the same divergence the moment a default ships an updated version: the file exists in the user's project from a prior install and never updates. The embedded note in FGAP-001's proposed_resolution surfaced this as 'decision pending' but the directional choice is not pending; the decision has been made and needs first-class reification so forward work does not continue down the already-decided-against path.
decisions.decision: pi-project does not ship default schemas or default block files. Users install desired schemas and starter blocks via an opt-in mechanism — concrete shape of the install mechanism is unspecified but the directional choice is final: no auto-seeding, no copy-on-init defaults shipped from the package. Bundled defaults are retired. This applies to all future schema and block kinds (config, relations, etc.) and to the existing default set under packages/pi-project/defaults/ once the opt-in mechanism replaces it.
consequences: issue-074's framing changes from 'fix seedExamples short-circuit' to 'the seeded artifacts must not exist; remove the source of the recurring class of issues'.

The substrate-arc subagent's recommended bundle 1 (Substrate seeding) is misframed: shipping config.schema.json + relations.schema.json + default config.json + default relations.json into packages/pi-project/defaults/ violates this decision. The POC's outputs ship as documentation/examples users copy, not as auto-seeded defaults.
packages/pi-project/defaults/ existing contents are grandfathered until the opt-in install mechanism is specified; no new content lands there.
initProject's default-copying loops at packages/pi-project/src/index.ts:215-236 are scheduled for retirement once the opt-in mechanism replaces them.

Future schema/block updates flow to users via the opt-in mechanism, not via package upgrades silently changing on-disk content. This eliminates the entire class of stale-seeded-file divergence issues at the source.
FGAP-001's proposed_resolution embedded note 'we might ship no defaults at all, and/or move defaults to a separate install step users enact if they want. Decision pending.' is superseded by this DEC.

references.label: Original embedded note in FGAP-001 proposed_resolution preserving user wording
pi-behavior-monitors live three-tier discovery (the retirement parallel)

decisions.title: Phase/item dependency authoring shape: inline depends_on with edge projection vs edges-only authoring
decisions.context: The roadmap/plan substrate proposal requires expressing per-phase ordering (phase A before phase B in a roadmap) and per-item ordering (task A before task B in a plan). DEC-0009 mandates closure-table edges as the canonical primitive for hierarchical decomposition, which covers ordering. Two authoring shapes are mandate-compliant under DEC-0009 — both ship ordering as edges; difference is the user-facing surface for declaring them. The proposal flagged this as a forking choice that user must resolve before implementation step 4 (loadRoadmap) lands.
decisions.decision: Edges-only authoring for both phase ordering and plan-item ordering. roadmap.schema.json's phases items omit depends_on entirely; plan.schema.json's items omit depends_on entirely. All ordering lives in relations.json with relation_type='phase_depends_on' (phases) and relation_type='plan_item_depends_on' (plan items). SDK reads and topologically sorts purely from edges. Authoring a dependency means appending an edge via append-block-item — identical to how lens-curate's curation-ceremony writes edges today. No inline syntactic-sugar field, no SDK projection logic, no inline-vs-edge sync, no drift diagnostic.

options_considered.label: Inline depends_on with SDK projection
options_considered.description: roadmap.schema.json's phases items + plan.schema.json's items each carry a depends_on: string[] field. At validation time, the SDK projects these to closure-table edges in relations.json with relation_type='phase_depends_on' or 'plan_item_depends_on'. Edges become the canonical storage; inline depends_on is authoring sugar. validateRoadmaps / validatePlans check inline-vs-edge consistency and emit drift diagnostics if they disagree.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: ergonomic authoring (depends_on lives where the phase is defined; one-place edits). Con: two surfaces for the same data; sync logic needed; drift surface; edge writes must check inline-syntactic-sugar fields too.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Introduces inline depends_on as a parallel storage primitive alongside the canonical closure-table edges, contradicting DEC-0009's principle of one storage primitive across all context types. Triggers the parallel-ungated-path antipattern (feedback_no_parallel_ungated_paths.md): the inline field becomes a writable surface alongside the canonical edges and requires the SDK to enforce sync, surfacing a roadmap_dep_drift diagnostic class that has no analogue under the edges-only path. Mutation cost is also higher: slotting a new plan into a roadmap requires nested-array surgery on the roadmap document AND a synced edge write; reordering requires per-affected-phase edits AND cascaded edge updates.

options_considered.label: Edges-only authoring
options_considered.description: roadmap.schema.json's phases items + plan.schema.json's items omit depends_on entirely. All ordering lives in relations.json with the new relation_types. Authoring a roadmap dependency means appending an edge via append-block-item with relation_type='phase_depends_on'; SDK reads + topo-sorts purely from edges.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: single source of truth; uniform with how lens-curate works (edges are the canonical write surface). Con: less local — to see what phase A depends on, must query relations.json under the relation_type filter.

consequences: Whichever option enacts becomes the canonical authoring contract for every future composition that needs ordering (sequence of decisions, dependency graph between research entries, etc.).

Edges-only option requires authoring tools (or a /project add-dep convenience subcommand) to make ordering authoring ergonomic from a session — append-block-item with the right relation_type is the canonical path.

Single source of truth for hierarchical/sequencing concerns honors DEC-0009. No new storage primitive introduced. validateRoadmaps + validatePlans diagnostic surface stays minimal — no roadmap_dep_drift / plan_dep_drift codes needed because no drift can occur.

Mutation patterns scale: slotting a new plan into a roadmap = one append-block-item on relations.json (atomic, schema-validated). Reordering plans = pure dependency-graph edit on relations.json (roadmap structure untouched). Same operation users already perform for lens curation.

Reading raw JSON requires a relations.json query to surface dependencies; this is irrelevant in practice because /project roadmap-view + project-roadmap-render assemble the full picture for display. Raw-JSON-reading is for authoring (per-edge), not for browsing.

Implementation impact: issue-081's roadmap.schema.json + plan.schema.json land WITHOUT depends_on fields on phases/items. issue-084 (loadRoadmap) + issue-085 (loadPlan) read ordering exclusively from getProjectContext(cwd).relations filtered by the new relation_types.

decisions.title: Edges-only authoring generalized: all inter-item relationships go through config-declared relation_types
decisions.context: DEC-0012 (enacted 2026-05-04) committed roadmap phase ordering and plan item ordering to edges in relations.json with hardcoded relation_type strings phase_depends_on and plan_item_depends_on. POC E (analysis/poc/pi-context-poc/E-relation-type-registry/, landed 2026-05-06 in commit 22a07a9) demonstrated mechanically that the same edges-only pattern works for any inter-item relationship type, and that the set of relation_types should be declared in config.relation_types[] with a category field (ordering | data_flow | membership) driving validator dispatch — not hardcoded as TS string constants in the SDK. The pi-context candidate articulation (analysis/2026-05-05-pi-context-executive-summary-candidate.md) names config-driven relation_types as a load-bearing primitive of the renamed substrate. DEC-0012's narrow scope leaves the substrate with edges-only ordering for two specific relationships and silently hardcoded everything else, mirroring the FGAP-013 antipattern that POC C/E close.
decisions.decision: All inter-item relationships in the typed-context substrate are authored as edges in relations.json with a relation_type field. The set of valid relation_types is declared in config.relation_types[] (one entry per kind, fields: canonical_id, display_name, category, cycle_allowed). Validators dispatch on category (ordering: topo sort + cycle check; data_flow: upstream-content-hash propagation; membership: parent-child set membership) via a CATEGORY_HANDLERS table keyed on category, not on relation_type name. Per-block schemas omit any inline depends_on / parent / consumes / member field that would parallel-store relationship data. SDK functions read the registry from config and emit per-category diagnostics. Adding a new relationship type is a config edit; no TypeScript change.
consequences: DEC-0012's scope (only phase_depends_on + plan_item_depends_on) is generalized to all inter-item relationships under any block kind. DEC-0012 is superseded by this decision.

ID_PREFIX_TO_BLOCK style hardcoded relation_type strings in pi-project/src/* (e.g. relation_type === 'phase_depends_on' branches) are antipattern under this decision; per-category dispatch tables key on category, not name.
FGAP-013 (status vocabulary registry hardcoded in TS) is the analogous gap on the status side; both close under the same config-driven-vocabulary architectural direction validated by POCs A/C/E.

Schema-side: roadmap.schema.json's phases items + plan.schema.json's items continue to omit depends_on (DEC-0012 contract preserved); future block kinds inherit the same omission as a substrate convention rather than a per-schema choice.
Adding a relation_type becomes a config.relation_types[] entry plus a category handler if a new category is introduced. Most additions reuse existing categories.
validateRelations() dispatches per category for diagnostics rather than emitting one undifferentiated diagnostic class.

This decision presumes the pi-context architectural direction (config-driven vocabulary substrate). Under continued pi-project naming with hardcoded vocabulary, this decision would not enact; it is coupled to that direction.

options_considered.label: Rewrite DEC-0012 with broader scope
options_considered.description: Edit DEC-0012 in place to extend its decision text and consequences to cover all relation_types.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Mutates an already-enacted decision in place. Erases the historical record of what DEC-0012 originally decided (narrow scope, two specific relation_types). Violates the lifecycle contract (open -> enacted; enacted is an integrity-preserving terminal state for that record). Rejected on integrity grounds.

options_considered.label: File DEC-0013 with broader scope; mark DEC-0012 superseded (this option)
options_considered.description: New decision filed with explicit supersedes: [DEC-0012]; DEC-0012 transitions enacted -> superseded with superseded_by: DEC-0013 and consequences appended explaining the broadening.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: preserves the historical decision record; uses the schema-supported supersession transition. Con: the schema status enum description for superseded says withdrawn without enactment / not-a-real-decision, which does not cleanly match enacted -> superseded for broadening. Stretching the semantic. File FGAP-015 (or next available) for the decision lifecycle gap (no broadened-by state).

decisions.title: Harness-confined main LLM; all action via extension tools, JIT-agent dispatch, and workflows; default Pi tools (bash/read/write/edit) excluded from the main thread
decisions.context: Empirical observation across this rebuild arc: every time the main LLM had access to bash/read/write/edit, it bypassed the substrate (direct file edits to .project/*.json instead of block-api; bash-grep instead of substrate query tools; write-to-disk instead of writeBlock). The bypasses recreated the F-006 trap (unrestricted tools + agentic model = tool-call loops + state corruption + parallel ungated paths) that FGAP-011 closure was filed to retire. The substrate-arc thesis (config-driven typed substrate as canonical surface) is structurally only realized when the substrate IS the operating surface, not one option among many. Tool-surface gap audit at analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md enumerates 22 gaps + meta-pattern (pi tool surface systematically lags JS export surface); under unconstrained-tools assumption these are nice-to-have architectural debt, but under harness-confined assumption they are functional spec items.
decisions.decision: Every Pi runtime invocation EXCLUDES bash/read/write/edit (the default Pi tools) from the main LLM's tool list. The main LLM operates ONLY through extension-registered tools (pi-context's 21 tools + pi-jit-agents / pi-workflows / pi-behavior-monitors registered tools). Out-of-substrate operations route through workflows that dispatch JIT agents whose .agent.yaml specs declare the required tool surface (bash/read/write/edit assignable per agent; spec-author authorizes; workflow step dispatches). The harness-confined model is the canonical execution surface for the typed-context substrate; unrestricted main-thread tools are explicitly rejected.
consequences: Tool-surface gap audit (analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md) is reframed from architectural debt to functional spec — closing the 22 enumerated gaps + meta-pattern is precondition for the harness-confined model to be operable. The 8-rank consolidated impact ranking becomes the implementation order.

FGAP-026 (config-driven bootstrap; substrate dir name from config) moves from P1 architectural-completeness to P0 unblocker — without read-config / read-schema / discovery tools, the harness-confined main thread cannot inspect the substrate it is meant to operate on. Bootstrap-edge hardcode is no longer 'embarrassing'; it is 'blocking'.

filter-block-items + resolve-items-by-id (the two practical-priority gaps from the audit) become P0 — without bash/grep, the main LLM has zero fallback for predicate-based queries beyond N-call serial probes against potentially-truncating read-block.

Subcommand-only surfaces (/project view, /project lens-curate, /project install, /project add-work) need either tool wrappers OR explicit JIT-agent dispatch contracts — the main LLM cannot invoke a subcommand from its tool list.
JS-export-only surfaces (schema-write, lens-view rendering, discovery aggregator, walk-ancestors, etc.) need tool wrappers — main LLM has no Read access to even inspect what those JS exports do.

Out-of-substrate operations (npm install, git status, file edits outside .project/, build/check/test invocations) reframe as workflow steps that dispatch JIT agents with appropriate tool surface. Main LLM cannot invoke npm run build directly; it dispatches a workflow.

Step 9 (adversarial audit, Task #11) shape changes — the audit can dispatch a JIT agent with read/grep tools, but the orchestrator consuming the audit report is harness-confined. Audit results need structured tool returns, not free-form report parsing.

Step 10 (release, Task #12) — npm publish + git tag + git reset --hard + git push remain user-execution per existing convention (interactive OTP, etc.). Agent-side build/check/test of step 1-8 must route through workflows that dispatch JIT agents with bash.

DispatchContext attestation (FGAP-004 closure) gains semantic weight — the WriterIdentity discriminator (human/agent/monitor/workflow) accurately reflects who took an action because the harness no longer permits ad-hoc bypass.

FGAP-024 (no write-authority enforcement) becomes more pressing — under harness-confined operation, blocking the main LLM from writing to inappropriate blocks at the dispatch boundary becomes the only enforcement layer.

feedback memory entries that gated bypass behavior (feedback_no_ad_hoc_code.md, feedback_no_destructive_git_ops.md, feedback_persist_means_mv_verbatim.md, feedback_tsx_eval_for_deterministic_state.md) describe behaviors the main LLM no longer has the surface to perform; they remain valid for JIT-agent-dispatched contexts.

Substrate-arc completion criterion (per FGAP-028, filed 2026-05-10): 'harness-confined dispatch dry-run' — synthetic main-LLM-as-tool-only invocation of representative arc work using ONLY extension tools + JIT-agent dispatch via workflows. Step 9 (adversarial audit, Task #11) gains a dogfood-dispatch test gate as part of its protocol. Step 10 (release, Task #12) is conditioned on Step 9's dogfood-dispatch passing. Tool-existence-as-completion-proxy (build/check/test gate) is necessary but insufficient under DEC-0014 because it hides agent-reachable surface gaps (e.g. 8 typed-file primitives from FGAP-019/020 closure ship as JS exports with no agent-reachable tool wrappers — invisible to the harness-confined consumer despite passing all builds/tests). FGAP-028 codifies the dogfood-completeness criterion; FGAP-026 closure + tool-surface gap audit closure are scoped against it.

options_considered.label: Default Pi tools available to main LLM
options_considered.description: Allow bash/read/write/edit as default tools; rely on instruction discipline (CLAUDE.md mandates, feedback memory) to prevent bypass.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Empirically rejected across the rebuild arc — every bypass observed (direct .project/*.json edits, bash-grep instead of resolve-item-by-id, write-to-disk instead of writeBlock) followed instruction-discipline framing. Mandate hooks remind but do not enforce. Recreates F-006 trap pattern. Continued substrate erosion as bypass becomes default-easier than canonical surface.

Con: tool-surface gap audit's 22 items + meta-pattern become P0 precondition; substantial implementation work to close before the model is operable; out-of-substrate operations require workflow authoring not direct execution.

decisions.title: Config drives substrate location; no hardcoded substrate-dir paths anywhere in pi-context, pi-jit-agents, pi-workflows, or pi-behavior-monitors
decisions.context: Step 2 substrate SDK port (commit d4ec3ce) commit message claimed 'config-driven vocabulary registries from line 1' but the bootstrap edge stayed hardcoded against `.project/` via PROJECT_DIR + SCHEMAS_DIR constants in packages/pi-context/src/project-dir.ts. config.root field exists in ConfigBlock but only relocates substrate state INSIDE the hardcoded outer dir; cannot relocate the outer dir itself. FGAP-026 enumerates the specific implementation gap. DEC-0014 (harness-confined main LLM) makes this a P0 unblocker because the harness-confined main thread cannot work around hardcoded paths via bash/cd/cat fallback. The principle ('config drives substrate location') is permanent substrate canon; the implementation gap (FGAP-026) closes when the resolver + cascades land. Anchor the principle separately from the gap so future work is checked against the principle, not just the closure status of FGAP-026.
decisions.decision: Substrate location is determined exclusively by config (read via a per-cwd resolver, e.g. resolveContextDir(cwd) reading a JSON-typed bootstrap pointer at <cwd>/.pi-context.json which carries the user-chosen substrate dir name; with default fallback). NO hardcoded substrate-dir paths anywhere in pi-context, pi-jit-agents, pi-workflows, pi-behavior-monitors source. Every path-construction site (block-api primitives, schema lookup, lens-view consumers, roadmap-plan, monitor side-car state, etc.) composes against the resolver. New tools and SDK functions take cwd as input + use the resolver; hardcoding `.project/` or `.context/` or any literal substrate dir name is a DEC-0015 violation requiring immediate remediation. /context init is prompt-required for the user's chosen dir name; defaulting to `.context/` reintroduces the hardcode-dressed-as-default pattern this decision rejects.
consequences: FGAP-026 is the current-state implementation-gap record for DEC-0015; FGAP-026 closes (status:closed + closed_by) when the resolver + cascades + bootstrap pointer schema + /context init + migration ceremony land. The DEC-0015 principle remains permanent canon after FGAP-026 closes — future tool/primitive additions are checked against DEC-0015 even with FGAP-026 in closed state.

Every new pi tool added to close the tool-surface gap audit (analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md) MUST take cwd as a parameter + use the resolver. Tools that hardcode any substrate dir name or accept a different scoping mechanism (e.g. global default substrate) violate DEC-0015.

Bootstrap pointer file (.pi-context.json) is the canonical lookup mechanism — schema authored under packages/pi-context/schemas/bootstrap.schema.json with $id pi-context://schemas/bootstrap + version 1.0.0 per FGAP-006 discipline. Pointer file is itself substrate (typed JSON with schema), not a sentinel.

Subcommand surface renames `/project` → `/context` (matches package name; reflects that the dir is config-driven, not name-bound); pi tool surface renames `project-*` → `context-*` (full rename; rebuild branch unpublished, zero downstream consumers).

Migration ceremony for existing `.project/`-using repos (currently this repo): `pi context migrate` renames `.project/` → `<user-chosen-name>/` (or accepts a `--keep .project` flag to preserve location while writing the bootstrap pointer). Default = rename to user-chosen.

All future Pi-runtime tooling outside pi-context (pi-jit-agents contextBlocks injection, pi-workflows substrate reads, pi-behavior-monitors block writes) imports the resolver from pi-context; no parallel resolution logic in any package.

The HANDOFF.md 'Bootstrap exemption: config.json + relations.json continue at <cwd>/.project/' line that surfaced FGAP-026 as LLM hedging is retired by this decision — there is no exemption; bootstrap is itself config-driven via the pointer mechanism.

DEC-0014 (harness-confined main LLM) gains structural backing: under harness confinement the main LLM cannot work around a hardcoded path via bash/cd/cat fallback, so DEC-0015 is the only mechanism enabling user-chosen substrate locations.

Documentation discipline (binding for all phases of FGAP-026 closure + all future tool/primitive additions): the framework-side config-mutability + user-content-prose-staleness dual-nature MUST be surfaced in EVERY documentation layer touching substrate-location: (a) code comments / docstrings at resolveContextDir, context-init, context-migrate, .pi-context.json schema; (b) CLAUDE.md project conventions; (c) root + per-package READMEs; (d) skill-narrative.md files (auto-loaded into LLM substrate consumers via SKILL.md regeneration); (e) any future tool that touches substrate-location resolution. The note states: framework-side rename is parameter-update with zero downstream breakage by design (context-migrate atomically renames dir + updates pointer + updates config.root); user-authored block content MAY contain hardcoded substrate-dir references in prose fields which the framework cannot detect or auto-rewrite — convention recommendation: prefer relative phrasing ('see decisions block entry X') over hardcoded paths ('.context/decisions.json line 47'). Filed as FGAP-027 (P3) for systematic tracking.

options_considered.label: Hardcoded `.project/` (or `.context/`) substrate dir; config.root only relocates within
options_considered.description: Keep PROJECT_DIR constant; treat 'bootstrap exemption' as legitimate design.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Empirically rejected — surfaced as FGAP-026; LLM rationalization in HANDOFF treated incomplete work as deliberate. Substrate-arc thesis structurally undelivered. Multi-substrate repos impossible. User-chosen dir name impossible. Recreates the hardcode-dressed-as-config pattern.

options_considered.label: Config-driven substrate location via per-cwd resolver + bootstrap pointer (this option)
options_considered.description: Replace PROJECT_DIR constant with resolveContextDir(cwd) reading .pi-context.json bootstrap pointer; cascade through all path-construction sites; no hardcoded substrate-dir anywhere.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: substrate-arc thesis structurally delivered; user-chosen dir name actually works; multi-substrate repos possible; tool-surface additions checked against the principle. Con: substantial implementation work (every path-construction site cascades); migration ceremony for existing .project/-using repos required; permanent vigilance against future hardcode regressions.

decisions.title: Same extension-tool surface consumed from both pi-runtime (harness-confined per DEC-0014) and Claude Code (instruction-imposed); direct .project/*.json edits forbidden in both surfaces
decisions.context: This session's empirical operating pattern — every FGAP filing + DEC filing + FGAP status transition + .project/ block content alignment (Step 8.5) + this very DEC filing went via npx tsx -e invocations against @davidorex/pi-context block-api primitives (appendToBlock / updateItemInBlock with DispatchContext attestation), not via Edit/Write/sed against .project/*.json files directly. The instruction-imposed convention has been operating throughout the rebuild arc; user direction this turn names it as the future-imposed convention to be substrate canon. DEC-0014 established surface-enforcement for pi-runtime (main LLM tool list excludes default Pi tools); DEC-0016 establishes the equivalent instruction-enforcement for Claude Code such that the SAME extension-tool surface is the consumption pattern from both execution surfaces. Tool-surface gap audit (analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md) is functional spec for both consumers; closing the 22 enumerated gaps + meta-pattern serves both pi-runtime + Claude Code.
decisions.decision: Claude Code sessions operating against pi-context substrate use the same extension-tool surface the harness-confined pi-runtime main LLM uses (per DEC-0014). Specifically: NO direct Edit / Write / sed / awk / cat-redirect targeting .project/*.json files. ALL substrate operations route through @davidorex/pi-context block-api primitives (writeBlock / appendToBlock / updateItemInBlock / upsertItemInBlock / removeFromBlock / nested variants / typed-file primitives) invoked via `npx tsx -e` inline scripts OR through future thin script wrappers (e.g. `bin/pi-context` CLI executable) that compose the same primitives. DispatchContext attestation required on every write (writer.kind = 'agent' for orchestrator-driven; writer.kind = 'human' for direct-user-driven; agent_id / user identifies the actor). Schema validation, proper-lockfile contention guard, per-field author stamping all flow through the canonical primitives — same semantics, same enforcement, regardless of which execution surface invoked. Pattern is enforced by instruction for Claude Code (retains bash/read/write/edit by harness design; cannot be surface-confined like pi-runtime under DEC-0014); operational equivalence is established by binding convention. Forbidden patterns explicitly: Edit on `.project/*.json`, Write on `.project/*.json`, Bash invocations using `sed -i` / `awk` / `cat > ` / `echo > ` / `>>` targeting `.project/*.json`. Optional thin script wrappers (`bin/pi-context <subcommand> --arg ...`) ship as ergonomic shell over block-api primitives — same primitives, same semantics, same DispatchContext requirement; no parallel ungated path.
consequences: Tool-surface gap audit (analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md) serves both consumers — closing the 22 enumerated gaps + meta-pattern unblocks both pi-runtime (DEC-0014 surface-enforced) and Claude Code (DEC-0016 instruction-imposed). Gap closure work is the same; consumer base doubles.

DispatchContext writer.kind on every block-api invocation: 'agent' for orchestrator-driven Claude Code work (agent_id = session identifier or task slug, e.g. 'post-step-8.6-orchestrator'); 'human' when direct-user-driven (user = david); 'monitor' / 'workflow' as before. Author attribution survives the invocation surface — every write is forensically attributable.

CLAUDE.md project conventions must surface this binding — DEC-0016 + DEC-0014 + DEC-0015 + DEC-0011 collectively define the substrate operational contract. Step 8.6 successor work or a documentation-discipline phase under FGAP-026 closure plan adds the binding statement: 'Claude Code sessions operate via extension tool surface invoked by `npx tsx -e` against block-api primitives OR future `bin/pi-context` wrappers; direct Edit / Write / sed / cat-redirect targeting .project/*.json is forbidden — same forbidden-pattern as pi-runtime under DEC-0014 surface enforcement'.

Cross-surface consistency check becomes natural Step 9 (adversarial audit) audit dimension: not just 'could harness-confined main LLM dispatch the arc?' (FGAP-028's dogfood-dispatch gate) but also 'could Claude Code session under DEC-0016's imposed pattern dispatch the arc using only the same extension tool surface?' Likely the same gaps surface in both; one surface check covers both; failure of either = blocking finding for Step 10 release.

DEC-0015's 9th consequence (documentation discipline for the dual-nature note) extends: code comments / docstrings / READMEs / skill-narratives surface the framework-config-mutability + user-content-prose-staleness caveat for BOTH pi-runtime consumers AND Claude Code consumers. Same documentation; both audiences read it.

Optional `bin/pi-context` CLI wrapper (NOT in scope for any specific phase yet; user-flagged for future authoring): ships as part of pi-context package; takes subcommand + JSON-shaped args (e.g. `pi-context append-block-item --block issues --array-key issues --item '{...}' --writer agent/<id>`); composes block-api primitives internally with DispatchContext construction; ergonomic alternative to inline tsx scripts. NOT a parallel ungated path because it's the same primitives.

Bypass under DEC-0016 is technically possible (Claude Code retains bash/read/write/edit) but explicitly forbidden by binding convention + harness-prepended-mandates + CLAUDE.md statement. Empirically: this rebuild arc's session has been dogfooding the convention all session; bypass observed at Step 8.5 origin (the 'Bootstrap exemption' framing as LLM rationalization of unaddressed work) was caught by user critique — instruction-imposed enforcement requires user-vigilance + structural mandate-prepend hooks; not surface-impossible like pi-runtime. The pattern is 'workable but requires the user-feedback loop' for Claude Code; surface-enforced for pi-runtime.

FGAP-024 (no write-authority enforcement layer beyond schema validation) gains weight from a second angle: under DEC-0016, Claude Code's 'agent' writer-kind has no upstream enforcement preventing writes to inappropriate blocks (e.g. agent writing to decisions.json that should be human-authored). Authority-matrix layer would gate cross-surface inappropriate writes regardless of execution-surface origin.

DEC-0014 + DEC-0015 + DEC-0016 form the canon trio establishing the substrate operational contract: surface enforcement for pi-runtime (DEC-0014); config drives location (DEC-0015); instruction-imposed equivalent for Claude Code (DEC-0016). All three required for substrate-arc thesis to be structurally complete.

Bypass forbidden by convention + mandate hooks + CLAUDE.md binding statement.

Con: instruction-imposed has weaker teeth than surface-imposed (bypass requires user-vigilance loop to catch + correct); Claude Code retains the technical capability to direct-edit; pattern relies on harness-prepended-mandate reminder + user-feedback enforcement; not as structurally airtight as DEC-0014's surface enforcement.

decisions.title: Work-unit context composed at dispatch time via bidirectional scoped traversal of config-declared relation_types per unit-kind context contract; inline reference arrays on work-units forbidden as DEC-0013 violation
decisions.context:
User-surfaced theoretical framing 2026-05-10: a unit-of-work is the convergence point of multiple context streams — intent (requirements, goals, issues being resolved), constraints (decisions, conventions, conformance rules, applicable architectural patterns), rationale, dependencies, touched-surfaces, verification criteria, authority/attribution, position in the larger arc. Forward edges are author-time (decision authored with enacted_by_phase pointer); backward edges are execution-time (task executor needs to walk back to all decisions constraining it). Without bidirectional traversal + composition primitives, work-unit dispatch requires N+1 reads + ad-hoc composition by the orchestrator before the executor can proceed. Under DEC-0014 + DEC-0016 (harness-confined main LLM + Claude Code instruction-imposed) the orchestrator cannot do that ad-hoc composition efficiently — work-unit context bundling must be a substrate primitive, not an orchestrator responsibility.

The specific articulation user critiqued (inline arrays like `related_requirements: string[]` on the task schema) violates DEC-0013 (edges-only authoring; all inter-item relationships go through config-declared relation_types in relations.json with category dispatch). The theoretical concept is correct (work-unit needs backward visibility to all relevant context streams); the implementation shape is closure-table edges + per-unit-kind context contracts + a composition primitive at gather-time, not inline reference arrays parallel-storing relationship data.
decisions.decision: Work-unit context (the bundle of items relevant to executing a unit-of-work) is composed at dispatch time by walking config-declared relation_types per a unit-kind-specific context contract. (1) RELATIONSHIPS: every link from a work-unit to context items is a closure-table edge in relations.json with a config-declared relation_type per DEC-0013 — NOT an inline array on the work-unit's schema. Reference-bearing fields like `related_requirements: string[]` on a task schema are forbidden as DEC-0013 violations; the substrate's authoritative reference graph lives in relations.json. (2) CONTEXT CONTRACT: per work-unit-kind, the substrate declares (in a context-contract block kind, OR encoded as composition-lens definitions in config.lenses[]) which relation_types compose the execution bundle. Different unit kinds have different bundles: implementation tasks need conventions + acceptance + architecture-touched; decision-making tasks need rationale + alternatives + related-decisions; verification tasks need verification-criteria + evidence-rules + items-under-verification. (3) GATHER PRIMITIVE: a single substrate operation (gather-execution-context(unitId, kind)) reads the unit + reads its context contract + walks the declared relation_types bidirectionally + composes the result as a single typed bundle returned as one structured payload. (4) DISPATCH-TIME INJECTION: pi-jit-agents' contextBlocks mechanism extends to item-level selectivity driven by gather-execution-context output — agents receive bundle as composed input, not as N whole-block dumps. (5) RELEVANCE SCOPING: traversal scoped per relation_type's category semantics; bounded by depth + applicability predicate (FGAP-010 territory) so reach-but-not-relevant items don't pollute the bundle.
consequences: Work-unit context bundling is a substrate primitive, not orchestrator responsibility — under DEC-0014/0016 the harness-confined main LLM cannot do ad-hoc N+1 reads to compose execution context efficiently; the substrate must deliver the composed bundle as one tool return.

DEC-0013 enforcement extends: inline reference arrays on ANY work-unit-kind schema (task, plan-item, story, phase, or future kinds) are forbidden. References to other items go through closure-table edges with config-declared relation_types. Migration of any existing schemas violating this (e.g. tasks.schema with inline depends_on, decisions.schema with inline related_findings, etc.) is its own arc — file as relevant FGAP if surfaced.

Per-unit-kind context contract is itself substrate data — either a new context-contract block kind declaring per-kind which relation_types compose the bundle, OR encoded as kind=execution-context-bundle composition lenses in config.lenses[] keyed by unit-kind. Latter reuses existing composition lens machinery (FGAP-012 closure surface); former adds a new block kind. Decision deferred to FGAP-030 closure work; either implementation satisfies DEC-0017.

FGAP cluster filed alongside this DEC: FGAP-029 (bidirectional scoped traversal primitive — walk-ancestors / gather-by-relation), FGAP-030 (per-work-unit-kind context contract substrate), FGAP-031 (gather-execution-context composition primitive), FGAP-032 (item-level contextBlocks selectivity in pi-jit-agents driven by gather output). FGAP-010 (applicability predicate language) supplies the relevance-scoping mechanism. FGAP-005 (state-machine validation) gates context-time validity. FGAP-024 (write-authority enforcement) extends to gather-time authority (who can request which bundles for which units).

Tool-surface gap audit (analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md) gains gather-execution-context as a P1 candidate alongside filter-block-items / read-config / read-schema. Under DEC-0014/0016 dogfood-completeness lens (FGAP-028) this primitive is required — without it, work-unit dispatch is undispatchable from a tools-only orchestrator.

Rebuild arc's FGAP-026 closure phase plan extends: a new phase between Phase 2 (high-impact query tools) and Phase 3 (workflow-execute) lands the gather-execution-context primitive + context contract substrate + walk-ancestors/walk-by-relation tools. Without this, Phase 4's arc-tracking-block authoring + Phase 5+ dispatch via dogfooding lacks the context-bundling surface.

Documentation discipline (DEC-0015 9th consequence) extends to DEC-0017 surface — gather-execution-context tool + context-contract block kind + per-unit-kind bundle semantics surface in code comments, CLAUDE.md, READMEs, skill-narratives.

DEC-0014 + DEC-0015 + DEC-0016 + DEC-0017 form the four-piece canon establishing the substrate operational contract: (a) surface enforcement for pi-runtime, (b) config drives location, (c) instruction-imposed for Claude Code, (d) work-unit context composed at dispatch time. All four required for substrate-arc thesis to be structurally complete; FGAP-028's dogfood-completeness gate measures all four together.

options_considered.label: Inline reference arrays on work-unit schemas (the user-quoted articulation)
options_considered.description: Add fields like related_requirements: string[], related_decisions: string[], etc. to tasks.schema (and parallel arrays to other work-unit-kind schemas). Each work-unit carries its own list of related-item IDs; reads are local to the unit.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Empirically rejected — violates DEC-0013 (parallel storage of relationship data alongside relations.json). Fragmentation: same relationship now lives in TWO places (the unit's array + relations.json edges) with no atomic write across both. Drift risk: array updated, edge missing, or vice versa. Reverse-traversal still requires reading all units to find inbound references (the array doesn't index in reverse). Cross-block consistency validation impossible because reference vocabulary scattered across N schemas. Same antipattern as FGAP-013's pre-DEC-0013 status-vocabulary-hardcoded-in-TS.

options_considered.label: Closure-table edges + per-unit-kind context contract + gather-execution-context composition primitive (this option)
options_considered.description: All work-unit→context references are edges in relations.json with config-declared relation_types per DEC-0013. Per-unit-kind context contract declares which relation_types compose the execution bundle. gather-execution-context(unitId, kind) primitive walks contract + bidirectionally traverses declared edges + composes one typed bundle. Substrate primitive serving harness-confined dispatch.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: substrate-arc thesis structurally consistent (single source of truth for relationships per DEC-0013); reverse traversal cheap (closure-table indexed); per-unit-kind contracts make context bundling extensible without schema changes per kind; orchestrator gets composed bundle in one tool call (FGAP-028 dogfood-dispatchable); attribution + state-machine + authority gates layer on naturally. Con: requires bidirectional traversal primitive (FGAP-029), context contract substrate (FGAP-030), composition primitive (FGAP-031), pi-jit-agents item-level injection (FGAP-032) — substantial closure work; relation_type registry must declare ALL the work-unit→context edge kinds (addresses, constrained_by, informed_by, depends_on, touches, verified_by, assigned_to, positioned_in or canonical naming aligned with substrate vocabulary).

decisions.title: Tests-pass alone is insufficient — every implementation step requires runtime demonstration + adversarial verification probe
decisions.context: LLMs perform. Tests passing does not mean the implementation does what it is supposed to do. Tests can pass for the wrong reason: a fixture-write side-effect masks the actual feature path; an assertion no longer tests what it claims; an import silently no-ops; a fallback swallows the failure. Observed concretely at FGAP-026 phase 1.2 ff13ff2 (committed broken code under false-green via pipe-mask exit code) AND at Section C.2 99436c3 (tests passed 150/0/1 but orchestrator could only verify cascade-shape from diff, not intent). User direction 2026-05-10: we cannot open ourselves to spending implementation and testing time only to find things do not work because LLMs do performance. Persisting as substrate-canon DEC because feedback memory alone is insufficient — LLMs choosing not to read memory must not be able to miss this gate.
decisions.decision: Every implementation step (sub-section commit, sub-phase commit, fix commit, feature commit) requires THREE verification layers, all mandatory, none skippable on grounds of small change or obvious correctness: (1) Tests pass — necessary, not sufficient; run with full output inspection (no pipe-mask). (2) Runtime demonstration — exercise the actual feature path end-to-end via real invocation (tsx eval against block-api / pi -p tool dispatch / direct CLI invocation against real substrate); not a mocked unit assertion. (3) Adversarial verification probe — fresh-context agent (Explore or general-purpose) reads implementation + runtime demo + probes for false-pass scenarios (assertion exercises right path? bypass via fallback? indirect import chain reaches new surface despite no-cascade classification?). Subagent briefs embed runtime-demo requirement; orchestrator dispatches adversarial probe as separate foreground subagent immediately after each implementation; status cascade includes runtime-demo result + adversarial verdict.
consequences: Sub-phase / sub-section completion sequence extends from edit + build + check + test + commit to edit + build + check + test + runtime-demo + adversarial-probe + commit + status-cascade. Per-step time cost increases proportional to demo + probe surface.
Plan-mode briefs explicitly enumerate the runtime demonstration shape per section. Plans without runtime-demo specs are incomplete.

Adversarial probe is a separate foreground subagent (general-purpose or Explore depending on probe type), not bundled with the implementation subagent. Per feedback_adversarial_audits_not_self_audits.
Status-cascade across 3 layers (Claude Code Tasks + .project/ blocks + HANDOFF.md) gains runtime-demo + adversarial-verdict fields, not just commit SHA + test exit code.

Existing implementations landed without runtime-demo + adversarial probe (Sections A bb2c7d5 / B 9846b90 / C.1 8f33a38 / C.2 99436c3 + audit-fix cluster) are subject to retroactive runtime-demo + adversarial verification when user directs.

Cross-references feedback_runtime_demo_plus_adversarial_per_step.md (binding doctrine layer) + feedback_pipe_masks_exit_code.md (concrete gate-discipline subset) + feedback_adversarial_audits_not_self_audits.md (probe-must-be-fresh-context).
Standing tension with implementation velocity: every step takes longer. Accepted as cost of correctness; tests-pass-alone optimization is the false-economy that DEC-0018 forecloses.
